channel.title: The Laughing Tree
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The Laughing Tree" - Greenlight? A tree manipulates children into hazing one of their own.

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Heyo followup!

SAND

+ show block
– hide block


How will you plan to show all of this in the narrative? currently there is only really interviews, but for some of this information it sounds like there would need to be some sort investigation or further tests.- The child wouldn't be able to show much outside of their own experiences.

Regarding your point about how the tree is called to the Foundation's attention. It's true that this is a little lacking. But I think that the foundation would be interested in rumors/tales that allude to a possible SCP entity. Perhaps the investigation department checks the background for names written on the tree and finds a pattern of anti-social behavior among them. Is there something in SCP that could possible help with this point? like a signature for a nefarious group or individual?

Hmm… I'm still kind of skeptical on this, the Foundation isn't likely (at least not to me), to take a scout tale of all things seriously enough to look further into it. Unless there is some sort evidence to show otherwise, I don't think the Foundation being curious will work.-do note that even if they did look at the tree/camp ground, they're likely not going to see anything immediately off. They would really need to know what they're looking for if they wanted something concrete.

-Perhaps you could work off this subtly a little bit? with it taking a while for the child to convince their parents and furthermore the Foundation that this is something more than a particularly bad camp experience?

That said;

like a signature for a nefarious group or individual?

You could go this route if you want to, however you would need to prepared to expand a bit more on the individual responsible for this. Along with showing a bit more on why the tree was created.

Due to my afore mentioned concerns, no-greenlight

If you would like me review this (or any concept) further, then please feel free to send me a PM or ask via IRC. Response times generally range from 2-12 hours over PMs. If your looking for additional critiques/perspectives in general, then I would highly recommend contacting the butterfly squad :)

Cheers,

RED

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Hi RED, Thank you for the review.

Some concept details couldn't be included since I hit the 400-word limit, but many of your points are valid. I'll address them below.

+ show block
– hide block

how exactly does the tree convince you to tie someone up?

It targets socially isolated kids. It frames the victim for theft. And this part was removed, but the tree also trips aggressive kids while they are near this victim. (Also, some members of the camp staff are in on it, as they are former victims)

does the tree itself carve the child's name? or do the children do it?

The tree does it.

how exactly do these victims live on to help the tree? do they still have any free will? what happens when they leave the camp? what exactly happens after ones name is carved?- in the immediate future?

They leave with a warped sense of empathy, and enjoy watching other kids go through the same ordeal. Some of them return as camp staff and help "stoke the fire" by laughing at the victim when he asks for help or by putting a stolen item back when the victim tries to get rid of it.

why the child didn't want to go to camp

Prior to camp, he was already socially isolated among peers.

what exactly happened after he got tied to the tree.

The interviewed child is not tied to the tree. He manages to stop its influence by wetting himself. One particular goal of this piece is to show that those that escape still feel shame and have a normal sense of empathy. The interviewed camp staff, however, was tied to the tree in his youth, and now laughs at the experience while disregarding the pain of others.

parts of the narrative that show the parent stepped in?

The parent is not present at the camp. He only steps in during the interview, when his child becomes distraught or to answer the few questions he can.

Regarding your point about how the tree is called to the Foundation's attention. It's true that this is a little lacking. But I think that the foundation would be interested in rumors/tales that allude to a possible SCP entity. Perhaps the investigation department checks the background for names written on the tree and finds a pattern of anti-social behavior among them. Is there something in SCP that could possible help with this point? like a signature for a nefarious group or individual?

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Hello Granite Mask! let's take a look :)

SAND

+ show block
– hide block

The concept:

Elevator Pitch: The Laughing Tree is a memetic entity rooted within an old wooden-tent campground commonly used by scouts or youth groups. The tree convinces children to tie one of their own to its trunk, and, when secured, it carves their name into its bark. These victims live on to help the tree find new prey, and laugh at the pain and suffering it inflicts.

Overall the premise sounds interesting, however, theres a few things that don't really add up to me (or aren't really explained/clarified). They're as follows;

-how exactly does the tree convince you to tie someone up?
-does the tree itself carve the child's name? or do the children do it?
-how exactly do these victims live on to help the tree? do they still have any free will? what happens when they leave the camp?
-what exactly happens after ones name is carved?- in the immediate future?

The narratives:

Central Narrative: A low-level foundation member calls attention to this entity after his child is almost tied to the tree. Previously, he laughed the tale off as a "camp story" to several co-workers. He is told to bring in his child for questioning.

This makes me a bit skeptical, the Foundation doesn't really have any real reason to believe this is an anomaly. Especially considering they how little context they have.

The first interiew is short. After providing a cover story, the investigator asks what happened. Emotionally dead, the kid replies, "you'll just laugh at me.."

The second interview occurs after the parent tries to console their child. The parent tries to help answer preliminary questions by describing camp activities. At the mention of knot-tying, the child's face goes pale.

The interviewer asks why the child didn't want to attend camp. Instead of answering he makes a small digression about how he felt sorry for a spider that another kid burned. The parent interrupts and describes his child as socially isolated and wonders if this behavior is related to the nature of his work.
Eventually, the child describes what happened, One-by-one belongings of other scouts find their way to his tent. Camp staff approach him and ask about thefts. The child tries to explain that he didn't take them, but they laugh at him. He runs. Then they, the scouts, surround him with knot-tying ropes and force him toward the *tree*. He tries and fails to escape, as the scouts laugh at him. The laughter grows louder.. and deeper until he realizes that it's coming from behind. And then, the laughter stops.
The investigator asks what changed. The child looks down, and cries.

A third interview takes place with a camp staff-member and former victim. He laughs at suffering he causes, and his own.

As it stands, it feels like I'm missing a fair amount of context here? there isn't really much of an answer to how/why the child didn't want to go to camp (were they aware something was wrong beforehand?), how/why items kept making their way to his tent (was this planned?), and what exactly happened after he got tied to the tree.- the narrative seems to imply his name got carved, but then there are also parts of the narrative that show the parent stepped in?

Red or green?
Due to my afore mentioned concerns, no-greenlight

If you would like me review this (or any concept) further, then please feel free to send me a PM or ask via IRC. Response times generally range from 2-12 hours over PMs. If your looking for additional critiques/perspectives in general, then I would highly recommend contacting the butterfly squad :)

Cheers,

RED

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Thank you.

And thanks for helping me spit out the poison pill.

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Ok, based on the revisions and your response I’m gonna greenlight this

BUT be very careful not to edge into grim dark or edgy tone… bullying is a serious topic, so don’t rush your way through it. Subtlety is your friend, and keep in mind how others might feel when reading it

Meaning don’t go for cheap SHOCK statements.

Good luck

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
I want to show the emotional consequences of hazing.

The 1st and 2nd interviews show the beginning: a child that is surrounded by peers who see him as "the other" and adults that laugh at him while doing nothing. But there is a difference here, he isn't irreparably scarred by the tree. He cries and feels shame about what happened.

The 3rd interview shows the worst possible end: an adult who feels pleasure and mirth by watching children go through the same experience he did. His name scarred the tree as it did him. When his pain is eventually revealed, he will laugh at it, just as he did during the child's "tying."

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Good edits, especially the hook; it's now an actual hook.

1) Disappearances could work. At the very least, it eliminates a connection being made. However, as much as I hate saying it, the tree does need some protein; if it's just making people suffer that weakens it.

2) You've already established that the tree controls the kids and counselors, so making them do minor things like framing another camper works. it also keeps to the theme.

3) You need to actually say it; it's a rare bit of humor, and you need it. It's also a neat subversion of how shameful self-urination is.

4) Cool.

5) The origins of the weakness helps to clarify things. Also, if it's a weakness, then it helps explain how they found it.

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
it causes them to laugh & enjoy the pain of others?

This sounds much more interesting than death or disappearing… like this anomaly reaching into your emotional state and changing the way your empathy works is intriguing. But then… what do you want to do with that? For example, what is the ultimate point of the story? Where do you want the arc to go? What are the themes you're working with? At the end of the story, where is it you want the reader to be?

I don't have much to say about the escape…

I think you need to think beyond the core anomaly and consider what your narrative is about. What is the overall point you're going for?

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
I agree.

Neither the name writing or suicide existed in the previous iteration. Instead, being tied to the tree meant disappearing weeks later. Plainly, I went from a cliche to something over the top.

I'm struggling with two parts of this concept:

  1. What happens after being tied to the tree
  2. How the survivor escaped
+ show block
– hide block

For the first, I want the names to be written on the tree. And I kinda want the victim to return to it. The name-writing is meant to represent the emotional scaring this event has on the victim. … What if the camp staff interview reveals that the he knew about the SCP and had even been one of it's victims in the past? Instead of killing/disappearing people, it causes them to laugh & enjoy the pain of others?

For the second, (at the moment) I just have the tree disgusted by urine and a victim that wets himself. To be clear, this would never be explicitly stated. I want the escape method to be embarrassing & uncomfortable, and an "accident" seems to fit. Any thoughts?

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
So my biggest concern is this part:

Many of these children later runaway from home, but some of them return to the tree, carve their name in its trunk and kill themselves.

My concern is two-fold:
a) using suicide (especially of children!) as shock value in a skip is so grim dark edgy that I lose interest immediately… using suicide in a skip needs to be handled delicately, and earning that edgy subject matter with some of substance. Here it feels unearned
b) the thing what which makes you crazy, and/or kill yourself, is such an overwrought trope on the wiki that I just tend to lose interest. The thing making you crazy/kill yourself really has to say something interesting/new or it’ll just feel cliched. I don’t see much here to suggest you’re pushing through the usual tropes of using such a compulsion effect on an anomaly.

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded: Made major edits to the elevator pitch, and minor edits to the rest of the concept.

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

I have a few ideas to address these problems, but some of them are unpolished.

+ show block
- hide block

1) I'm thinking that the tree doesn't physically consume the child during the ritual. This would prevent alarm from camp disappearances. But that doesn't mean there aren't more concrete consequences. One of the post-notes would mention that a child from each camp period would suddenly disappear days or weeks after attending camp. Disappearances like this are often written off as runaways or kidnappings and would largely be unattributed to the camp.

Alternatively, the tree could replace the kids with something else, but I think either option is best left unexplained.

2) I have two ideas for object manipulation. The tree could use memetic influence to have kids do the framing themselves, or it could use its roots to physically move things. This may allow other incidents such as tripping kids near the victim. I'm not sure what would be better, as the root network might open up a can of worms, but memetic influence seems to write away the cruelty as "not their fault." I kind of like the idea of people doing horrible things under the right circumstances.

3) The tree is disgusted by urine, ending the ritual/laughter. I want to convey that the kid wet himself without actually saying it. I feel like this could be horrifying & funny depending on the audience, which also plays into the whole "laughing it off" theme.

4) Thanks! :) I have some ideas for connecting this moment to the rest of the story.

5) I think I assumed that the hook had to be in the beginning of the article (i.e. the containment procedure). Falling back to #3, the audience would wonder why human urine is involved at all and jump back to the beginning to make the connection. But the first interview might be a better hook for the overall narrative and laughing trope.

EDIT: Shortened my reply.

item.title: Re: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
I like this tree. It's got that cool mix of campfire story and actual hazard.

1) The big question is how the tree hides the fact that a child died while under relatively close supervision. You're going to have to explain what appears to be a kidnapping to the authorities (presumably the child's belongings are still at camp) while no one is filming. That's something that can shut down a camp and create a lot of problems.

2) The tree is capable of manipulating objects? A better way of doing it would be that a kid drops them off at the tent in question and then forgets about it. However, I'd point out that the counselors are going to point out that the tents are not exactly secure; that lack of security is why you see so many pranks during those camping events. It also means that just as anything can be stolen from them, so is it easy to frame someone.

All of this means that the tree has more than just a fancy way of being invisible but also has a level of mind control going on as well.

3) If not all of its victims are acceptable, then you need to tell why it lets some of them go. The fewer mysteries the better.

4) The part about the spider was a nice touch, especially as the kid has a reason to feel empathy with spider.

5) The Hook: You need to add that to the Central Narrative. A "hook" is why we should basically care for it, and what makes it unique, or even works into the greater universe; you have a great hook in that this is a situation where laughter becomes something to be feared, which is sort of cool.

Again, a great idea, it just has some minor problems.

item.title: The Laughing Tree
item.content:encoded:
Seeking Greenlights: Yes

Page Type: SCP Article

Genre (Optional): Campfire Horror, Emotional

Page Layout (Optional): Containment procedure, description of the entity's feeding ritual, 2 interviews with a survivor of the ritual, and 1 interview with an involved camp staff-member.

Elevator Pitch: The Laughing Tree is a memetic entity rooted within an old wooden-tent campground commonly used by scouts or youth groups. The tree convinces children to tie one of their own to its trunk, and, when secured, it carves their name into its bark. These victims live on to help the tree find new prey, and laugh at the pain and suffering it inflicts.

Central Narrative: A low-level foundation member calls attention to this entity after his child is almost tied to the tree. Previously, he laughed the tale off as a "camp story" to several co-workers. He is told to bring in his child for questioning.

+ show block
– hide block

The first interiew is short. After providing a cover story, the investigator asks what happened. Emotionally dead, the kid replies, "you'll just laugh at me.."

The second interview occurs after the parent tries to console their child. The parent tries to help answer preliminary questions by describing camp activities. At the mention of knot-tying, the child's face goes pale.

The interviewer asks why the child didn't want to attend camp. Instead of answering he makes a small digression about how he felt sorry for a spider that another kid burned. The parent interrupts and describes his child as socially isolated and wonders if this behavior is related to the nature of his work.

Eventually, the child describes what happened, One-by-one belongings of other scouts find their way to his tent. Camp staff approach him and ask about thefts. The child tries to explain that he didn't take them, but they laugh at him. He runs. Then they, the scouts, surround him with knot-tying ropes and force him toward the *tree*. He tries and fails to escape, as the scouts laugh at him. The laughter grows louder.. and deeper until he realizes that it's coming from behind. And then, the laughter stops.

The investigator asks what changed. The child looks down, and cries.

A third interview takes place with a camp staff-member and former victim. He laughs at suffering he causes, and his own.

Hook/Attention-Grabber: Laughter, not as a coping mechanism but as something to be feared.

Additional Notes: Besides names, the tree also has "HA" etched into its bark, and it is disgusted by urine.
